Unit 3 Project

Rachel Ford

2026-03-01

library(tidyverse)
library(ggplot2)
library(GGally)
library(MASS)
library(klaR)

Part I: Morphological Data

Question 1

Answer: The trait means for each species are petal width for setosa(smallest average), petal length for versicolor (middle most averages), and sepal length(largest average) for virginica. The significant differences were only found for setosa as the other two closely overlapped.

Iris_Means <- iris %>%
group_by(Species) %>%
summarize(mean(Sepal.Width), mean(Sepal.Length), mean(Petal.Length), mean(Petal.Width))

table.mean <- data.frame(Iris_Means)
table.mean
##      Species mean.Sepal.Width. mean.Sepal.Length. mean.Petal.Length.
## 1     setosa             3.428              5.006              1.462
## 2 versicolor             2.770              5.936              4.260
## 3  virginica             2.974              6.588              5.552
##   mean.Petal.Width.
## 1             0.246
## 2             1.326
## 3             2.026

Question 2

ggplot(iris, aes(x = Petal.Length, y = Petal.Width, color = Species)) + geom_point() + labs(title = "Edgar Anderson's Iris Data",
                                                                                            x = " Petal Length", y = "Petal Width") + theme_bw()

ggplot(iris, aes(x = Petal.Length, y = Sepal.Length, color = Species)) + geom_point() + labs(title = "Edgar Anderson's Iris Data",
                                                                                            x = " Petal Length", y = "Sepal Length") + theme_bw()

ggplot(iris, aes(x = Petal.Length, y = Sepal.Width, color = Species)) + geom_point() + labs(title = "Edgar Anderson's Iris Data",
                                                                                            x = " Petal Length", y = "Sepal Width") + theme_bw()

ggplot(iris, aes(x = Petal.Width, y = Petal.Length, color = Species)) + geom_point() + labs(title = "Edgar Anderson's Iris Data",
                                                                                            x = " Petal Width", y = "Petal Length") + theme_bw()

ggplot(iris, aes(x = Petal.Width, y = Sepal.Length, color = Species)) + geom_point() + labs(title = "Edgar Anderson's Iris Data",
                                                                                            x = " Petal Width", y = "Sepal Length") + theme_bw()

ggplot(iris, aes(x = Petal.Width, y = Sepal.Width, color = Species)) + geom_point() + labs(title = "Edgar Anderson's Iris Data",
                                                                                            x = " Petal Width", y = "Sepal Width") + theme_bw()

ggplot(iris, aes(x = Sepal.Length, y = Petal.Length, color = Species)) + geom_point() + labs(title = "Edgar Anderson's Iris Data",
                                                                                            x = " Sepal Length", y = "Petal Length") + theme_bw()

ggplot(iris, aes(x = Sepal.Length, y = Petal.Width, color = Species)) + geom_point() + labs(title = "Edgar Anderson's Iris Data",
                                                                                             x = " Sepal Length", y = "Petal Width") + theme_bw()

ggplot(iris, aes(x = Sepal.Length, y = Sepal.Width, color = Species)) + geom_point() + labs(title = "Edgar Anderson's Iris Data",
                                                                                             x = " Sepal Length", y = "Sepal Width") + theme_bw()

ggplot(iris, aes(x = Sepal.Width, y = Petal.Length, color = Species)) + geom_point() + labs(title = "Edgar Anderson's Iris Data",
                                                                                             x = " Sepal Width", y = "Petal Length") + theme_bw()

ggplot(iris, aes(x = Sepal.Width, y = Petal.Width, color = Species)) + geom_point() + labs(title = "Edgar Anderson's Iris Data",
                                                                                            x = " Sepal Width", y = "Petal Width") + theme_bw()

ggplot(iris, aes(x = Sepal.Width, y = Sepal.Length, color = Species)) + geom_point() + labs(title = "Edgar Anderson's Iris Data",
                                                                                            x = " Sepal Width", y = "Sepal Length") + theme_bw()

Question 3: Using only two of each sets of measurements on irises and discriminant analyses, which two of them give the best predictions of species as scored by Edgar Anderson? How much do these two sets of measurements differ, and how do they compare with the other sets of measurements?

Answer: The two sets that give the best predictions are petal.width vs. sepal.width and petal.length vs. sepal.length. These sets of measurements have the same app. error rate of 0.033, which is the lowest of the other options, with the next app. error rate at 0.04.

ggplot(iris, aes(x = Petal.Width, y = Sepal.Width, color = Species)) + geom_point() + labs(title = "Edgar Anderson's Iris Data",
                                                                                           x = " Petal Width", y = "Sepal Width") + theme_light()

ggplot(iris, aes(x = Petal.Length, y = Sepal.Length, color = Species)) +
  geom_point() + labs(title = "Edgar Anderson's Iris Data",
                      x = "Petal Length", y = "Sepal Length") + theme_light()

Question 4:What data did you use to evaluate which two measurements gave the best predictions of species? Based on this data, are you confident in Edgar Anderson’s predictions? Why or why not?

###Answer: The data that I used was the LDA data displaying all of the plots from the example used in lecture. I saw which two had the lowest app. error rates and then created new data sets with petal.length vs. sepal.length and petal.width vs. sepal width and ran new LDAs for each. Based on the low error rates, I am confident in Anderson’s predictions.

Part II: Geographical and temporal data

library(tidyverse)
library(ggplot2)
library(maps)
library(dismo)
get.gbif <- function (genus, species, na.rm=TRUE, crop=TRUE)
{
  #GBIF query
  gps <- gbif(genus, species, geo=FALSE) 
  #drop NA entries; note that this option is visited only with na.rm=TRUE
  if(na.rm){gps <- subset(gps, !is.na(lon) & !is.na(lat))}
  if(crop){
    keep <- c(which(colnames(gps)=="species"),which(colnames(gps)=="lat"),which(colnames(gps)=="lon"),
              which(colnames(gps)=="month"))
    gps <- gps[keep]
  }
  return(gps)
}

iris_gps <- read_csv("iris_gps.csv")

write_csv(iris_gps, file = "iris_gps.csv", col_names = TRUE)

ggplot2::theme_set(theme_bw())

world.map <- map_data(map = "world")
us.map <- map_data(map = "usa")

Question 1

Answer: It appears that most of this subspecies of rattlesnakes have been observed in Florida.

snake_gps <- read_csv("0005347-260226173443078.csv")

rattlesnakes <- get.gbif("Sistrurus", "miliarius")

write_csv(rattlesnakes, file = "0005347-260226173443078.csv", col_names =  TRUE)


ggplot(world.map, aes(long, lat, group = group)) + geom_polygon(fill = "white", colour = "grey50") + coord_quickmap() + geom_point(data = snake_gps,
                                                                                                                                   mapping = aes(x = lon, y = lat, group = species, color = species), size = 0.5)

ggplot(us.map, aes(long, lat, group = group)) + geom_polygon(fill = "white", colour = "grey50") + coord_quickmap() + geom_point(data = snake_gps,                                                    mapping = aes(x = lon, y = lat, group = species, color = species), size = 0.5) + labs(title = "Pygmy Rattlesnakes")

Question 2

us_long <- range(us.map$long, na.rm = TRUE)
us_lat <- range(us.map$lat, na.rm = TRUE)

iris_zoom <- iris_gps %>%
  filter(lon >= us_long[1], lon <= us_long[2], lat >= us_lat[1], lat <= us_lat[2])

ggplot(us.map, aes(long, lat, group = group)) + geom_polygon(fill = "white", colour = "grey50") + geom_point(data = iris_zoom, mapping = aes(x = lon, y = lat, group = species, color = species), size = 0.5) + labs(title = "Iris in the U.S.A")

Question 3

ne_lat <- c(38, 48)
ne_long <- c(-85, -65)
ne_iris <- iris_zoom %>%
  filter(lon >= ne_long[1], lon <= ne_long[2], lat >= ne_lat[1], lat <= ne_lat[2])

ggplot(us.map, aes(long, lat, group = group)) + geom_polygon(fill = "white", colour = "grey50") + geom_point(data = ne_iris, mapping = aes(x = lon, y = lat, group = species, color = species), size = 0.5, inherit.aes = FALSE) + coord_cartesian(xlim = ne_long, ylim = ne_lat) + labs(title = "Iris in the Northeast U.S.A")

Question 4

Answer: The distribution is that while the iris species are all over, versicolor and viginica are primarily overlapping most frequently and setosa is mostly in the northwestern US alone. It seems that the species each have some shared preference for colder environments, but one could argue that there is a geographic separation of iris setosa. This proposes the idea of temporal reproductive differentiation.

Question 5

###Answer: This is not a very useful way to see if these are truly different species. In some ways,it can be observed when the flowers are blooming, it does not account for where the irises are in proximity to each other or geographically in regards to climate. Another challenge is that this is only for one year, but there are seasonal changes over the course of years- specifically El Nino and La Nina years, where there is more precipitation at different times of the year.

iris.months <- iris_gps %>%
  filter(!is.na(month), month >= 1, month <= 12)

ggplot(data = iris.months, aes(x = month, y = after_stat(density), group = species, fill = species)) + geom_histogram(position = "dodge",
                                                                                                                   binwidth = 1) + facet_wrap(~species, nrow = 3) + scale_x_continuous(breaks = 1:12, labels = month.abb, limits = c(1, 12)) + labs(title = "Iris Data Observed By Months") + theme_minimal()
## Warning: Removed 6 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
## (`geom_bar()`).

Part III: Chromosomal data

iris_chrom_dataset <- read_csv("iris_chromosomes.csv")
## Warning: One or more parsing issues, call `problems()` on your data frame for details,

##   problems(dat)
head(iris_chrom_dataset)

##   CCDB is a community resource. If you are aware of any missing data or if any…¹
##   <chr>                                                                         
## 1 "id,source,internal_id,major_group,family,genus,original_name,matched_name,re…
## 2 "ipcn-api-dl-53132,ipcn-api-dl,16600207,Angiosperms,Iridaceae,Iris,Iris acuti…
## 3 "book-atlas-flowering-plants-11026,book-atlas-flowering-plants,,Angiosperms,I…
## 4 "ipcn-api-dl-53159,ipcn-api-dl,16600226,Angiosperms,Iridaceae,Iris,Iris ewban…
## 5 "ipcn-api-dl-53194,ipcn-api-dl,16600242,Angiosperms,Iridaceae,Iris,Iris lineo…
## 6 "ipcn-api-dl-53195,ipcn-api-dl,16600242,Angiosperms,Iridaceae,Iris,Iris lineo…
## # ℹ abbreviated name:
## #   ¹​`CCDB is a community resource. If you are aware of any missing data or if any of the counts are erroneous please submit it to mailto:evolseq@tauex.tau.ac.il`
iris_chrom_dataset <- read_csv("iris_chromosomes.csv", skip = 1)

head(iris_chrom_dataset)
## # A tibble: 6 × 15
##   id      source internal_id major_group family genus original_name matched_name
##   <chr>   <chr>        <dbl> <chr>       <chr>  <chr> <chr>         <chr>       
## 1 ipcn-a… ipcn-…    16600207 Angiosperms Irida… Iris  Iris acutilo… Iris acutil…
## 2 book-a… book-…          NA Angiosperms Irida… Iris  Iris acutilo… Iris acutil…
## 3 ipcn-a… ipcn-…    16600226 Angiosperms Irida… Iris  Iris ewbanki… Iris ewbank…
## 4 ipcn-a… ipcn-…    16600242 Angiosperms Irida… Iris  Iris lineola… Iris lineol…
## 5 ipcn-a… ipcn-…    16600242 Angiosperms Irida… Iris  Iris lineola… Iris lineol…
## 6 ipcn-a… ipcn-…    16602561 Angiosperms Irida… Iris  Iris acutilo… Iris acutil…
## # ℹ 7 more variables: resolved_name <chr>, taxonomic_status <chr>,
## #   gametophytic <chr>, sporophytic <chr>, parsed_n <chr>, reference <chr>,
## #   voucher <lgl>
iris_chrom <- iris_chrom_dataset %>%
  dplyr::select(resolved_name, sporophytic, parsed_n) %>%
  separate(resolved_name, into = c("genus", "species"), sep = " ", remove = TRUE, extra = "drop")

head(iris_chrom)
## # A tibble: 6 × 4
##   genus species   sporophytic parsed_n
##   <chr> <chr>     <chr>       <chr>   
## 1 Iris  acutiloba 20          10      
## 2 Iris  acutiloba 20          10      
## 3 Iris  acutiloba 20          10      
## 4 Iris  acutiloba 20          10      
## 5 Iris  acutiloba 20          10      
## 6 Iris  acutiloba 20          10
setosa_chrom <- iris_chrom %>%
  filter(species == "setosa")

setosa_chrom
## # A tibble: 17 × 4
##    genus species sporophytic parsed_n

virginica_chrom <- iris_chrom %>%
  filter(species == "virginica")

virginica_chrom

versicolor_chrom <- iris_chrom %>%

versicolor_chrom

Question 1

iris_mode <- iris_chrom %>%
  group_by(species) %>%
  summarize(mode(iris_chrom), paste(
    unique(sporophytic), collapse = " "
  ))
head(iris_mode, 20)

Question 2

Question 3

Part IV: Producing a scientific argument

Question 1

###Answer: The most compelling piece of data would be the chromosomal data, with geographic being a close second. Chromosomal differences show that these are not able to reproduce due to the polyploidy present. Using this information, it can be said that there are three seperate species present- supporting Anderson’s designation. The additional data of the geographic and temporal data detracted from this conclusion, because both versicolor and virginica were very similiar in where they wer observed geographically. Simply going off of temporal data would also elude to two species based on overlap. In conclustion, I believe that these are truly three different species.
